title: Forest Admin API keys should not be disclosed
description:
Forest Admin requires two mandatory credentials per agent deployment:

- **envSecret** (`FOREST_ENV_SECRET`): A 64-character lowercase hexadecimal string that identifies and authenticates the agent with Forest Admin servers. Exposing it allows attackers to impersonate the agent and cause denial-of-service against the admin panel.
- **authSecret** (`FOREST_AUTH_SECRET`): A high-entropy random string used to sign JWT tokens issued to end-users of the admin panel. Exposing it allows attackers to forge authentication tokens and impersonate any user who has access to the admin panel, gaining full visibility into and control over your internal business data.

Both credentials are typically injected through environment variables (`process.env.FOREST_ENV_SECRET`, `process.env.FOREST_AUTH_SECRET`). Hardcoding these values in source code or configuration files committed to version control is a critical security risk because:

1. Anyone with repository read access can extract the credentials.
2. An exposed `authSecret` enables unlimited user impersonation on the admin panel.
3. Secrets are rarely rotated after an accidental commit, prolonging the exposure window.

Always load Forest Admin credentials from environment variables or a secrets manager and never embed literal key values in source code.
recommendation: Remove hardcoded Forest Admin credentials from source code immediately. Rotate both the envSecret and authSecret in the Forest Admin UI. Store the new values in environment variables (e.g. using a .env file excluded from version control, or a secrets manager such as AWS SSM, HashiCorp Vault, or Azure Key Vault) and reference them via process.env.FOREST_ENV_SECRET / process.env.FOREST_AUTH_SECRET.
impact: Exposure of authSecret allows attackers to forge admin-panel JWT tokens and impersonate any user, granting unauthorized access to all internal business data managed through Forest Admin. Exposure of envSecret enables agent impersonation and potential denial of service.

compliant.description: Credentials loaded from environment variables — the correct approach
Credentials loaded via os.environ in a Python settings file
compliant.code:
import os

# settings.py - Compliant
FOREST_AUTH_SECRET = os.environ['FOREST_AUTH_SECRET']
FOREST_ENV_SECRET  = os.environ['FOREST_ENV_SECRET']

checks.description: Detects hardcoded Forest Admin envSecret (FOREST_ENV_SECRET, 64-char hex) and authSecret (FOREST_AUTH_SECRET, high-entropy random string) in source code. Uses context-based matching: explicit FOREST_ENV_SECRET / FOREST_AUTH_SECRET env-var names are flagged when assigned a literal value; the shorter config keys (envSecret / authSecret) are only flagged when Forest Admin context signals are also present in the file. Env-var references (process.env.X, os.environ, etc.) are always excluded.
